---
title: "01 · What is an MDP? Grid-World Foundations"
subtitle: "Introduces the Markov Decision Process via the grid-world example used throughout the track."
lesson-id: "mfrl.mdp-and-grid-world"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["t3.basic-probability", "t5.sets-logic-proofs"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest. Are you ready for this lesson?

1. Can you write a [set](/glossary.qmd#set) like $\{a, b, c\}$ and say what
   "$a \in S$" means?
2. Can you read $P(A) = 0.3$ as "the [probability](/glossary.qmd#axiomatic-probability)
   of event $A$ is 30%"?
3. Are you comfortable with a small lookup table — rows for inputs, columns
   for outputs?

If a "no" appears, take ten minutes with
[T3 — Basic Probability](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/13-basic-probability/index.qmd)
or [T5 — Sets, Logic, Proofs](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)
before continuing.
:::

## Touch

Imagine a 5-by-5 board, drawn on graph paper. One square is **green** — your
target. One square is **red** — a pit you want to avoid. The other twenty-three
are blank. A small token sits on one of the blank squares.

Picture the rules of the game written on an index card:

- Each turn, you push the token one step **up**, **down**, **left**, or
  **right**.
- If you walk off the board, the token bounces back where it was.
- If you land on green, the game gives you **+1**. Land on red, **−1**. Any
  other square, **0**.

That is it. No vocabulary yet — just a game on a grid.

```{ojs}
viewof gridDemo = {
  const w = 320, n = 5;
  const cell = w / n;
  const target = [4, 4], forbid = [2, 2];
  const svg = DOM.svg(w, w);
  const g = d3.select(svg);
  for (let r = 0; r < n; r++) {
    for (let c = 0; c < n; c++) {
      const fill = (r === target[0] && c === target[1]) ? "#bbf7d0"
        : (r === forbid[0] && c === forbid[1]) ? "#fecaca" : "#f8fafc";
      g.append("rect").attr("x", c * cell).attr("y", r * cell)
        .attr("width", cell).attr("height", cell)
        .attr("fill", fill).attr("stroke", "#94a3b8");
    }
  }
  g.append("circle").attr("cx", 0.5 * cell).attr("cy", 0.5 * cell)
    .attr("r", cell * 0.25).attr("fill", "#1e3a8a");
  return svg;
}
```

Caption: a 5×5 grid with start (top-left blue token), target (green), and
forbidden cell (red). Every other cell is blank.

Push your finger from cell to cell and play a few turns mentally. You may not
yet know the *purpose* of the game — but you can already make moves.

## Wonder

Sit with these. No answers yet.

1. To decide your next push, what information do you actually need? The square
   you're standing on? The whole list of squares you've visited? Something
   else?
2. Suppose the floor is mildly slippery — sometimes "push up" lands you in the
   square you wanted, sometimes one to the side. Can you still plan?
3. The +1 lives on green and the −1 on red. Could two players following
   different turn-by-turn habits both reach the green square — but one of them
   collect more total +1s along the way?

## Discover

Five problems. Work them in order — each is a piece of the definition that
follows.

### Problem 1 — List the playing field

Number the rows and columns of a 3×3 grid from 0 to 2. Write the 9 cells as
ordered pairs $(r, c)$, collected into a single set $S$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A set just means "list the elements once each, separated by commas, inside
braces."
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$S = \{(0,0), (0,1), (0,2), (1,0), (1,1), (1,2), (2,0), (2,1), (2,2)\}$.

This $S$ is what we will later call the **state space** — the collection of
every situation the agent can find itself in.
</details>

### Problem 2 — List the moves

For the cell $(1, 1)$ (the centre), list every move you might try. Then for
the cell $(0, 0)$ (a corner), list the same set of *attempted* moves — even
the ones that bounce off the edge.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Moves you *attempt* and moves that *succeed* are different things. List the
attempts.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = \{\text{up}, \text{down}, \text{left}, \text{right}\}$, the same four
attempts at every cell. From $(0, 0)$, "up" is legal to attempt — it just
leaves you where you started. We deliberately keep $A$ the same at every
state; it makes the maths uniform later.

This $A$ is the **action space**.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Apply a clean rule

Define "up" as: $(r, c) \mapsto (r - 1, c)$, but if $r = 0$ then stay at
$(r, c)$. Starting at $(1, 2)$, where do you land after "up"? After "up,
up"? After "up, up, up"?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(1, 2) \xrightarrow{\text{up}} (0, 2) \xrightarrow{\text{up}} (0, 2)
\xrightarrow{\text{up}} (0, 2)$. The bounce-back keeps you safely inside
$S$.

Notice: the next cell only needed the **current cell** and the **chosen
move** — nothing about how you got to $(1, 2)$ in the first place.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Add a slip

Now make the floor slippery. "Up" still *intends* $(r, c) \mapsto (r-1, c)$,
but the actual outcome is

- intended cell with probability $0.8$,
- one step left of intended with probability $0.1$,
- one step right of intended with probability $0.1$.

Starting at $(1, 1)$ with action "up", list every cell you could end up in
and its probability. Verify the probabilities sum to 1.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Three possible next cells, three probabilities. Add them.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $(0, 1)$ with probability $0.8$ (intended).
- $(0, 0)$ with probability $0.1$ (left of intended).
- $(0, 2)$ with probability $0.1$ (right of intended).

Sum: $0.8 + 0.1 + 0.1 = 1.0$. Good. We have just written
$P(s' \mid s = (1,1), a = \text{up})$ as a row of a probability table.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Add a payout

Add the rule: landing on green pays +1; landing on red pays −1; anything
else pays 0. Take green = $(2, 2)$ and red = $(1, 2)$. From $(2, 1)$
attempt "right" (deterministic). What is the immediate reward?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
"right" from $(2, 1)$ lands you at $(2, 2)$, which is green. Reward = +1.

The reward depends on where you started, what you tried, and where you
ended — three pieces of information. We will write that as $R(s, a, s')$.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
The whole game is captured by **five things**: the cells $S$, the moves $A$,
the slip-rule $P$ that says where each move actually lands, the payout
rule $R$, and a number $\gamma$ that we will meet in lesson 4. Once those
five are pinned down, *every* question about how to play well is now a maths
question.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **Markov Decision Process** (MDP) is a tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$ where
$S$ is a [set](/glossary.qmd#set) of *states*, $A$ is a set of *actions*,
$P(s' \mid s, a)$ is a [conditional probability](/glossary.qmd#conditional-probability)
giving the next-state distribution, $R(s, a, s')$ is the *immediate reward*,
and $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the *discount factor*.

It is called *Markov* because $P$ and $R$ depend only on the **current**
state-action pair — never on the history.

:::

A verse to remember it by:

> *States, actions, kernel, reward, discount —*
> *five tokens, the world is bounded.*
> *Memory hides in the state alone;*
> *the past, once spoken, is rounded.*

## Apply

### Worked example — write the MDP for a 3×3 grid

Take the 3×3 grid with green at $(2, 2)$ and red at $(2, 1)$. Movement is
**deterministic**: each push goes where intended unless it would leave the
board, in which case the agent stays put.

**Step 1 — write $S$.** $S = \{(0,0), \ldots, (2,2)\}$, nine cells.

**Step 2 — write $A$.** $A = \{\text{up}, \text{down}, \text{left}, \text{right}\}$.

**Step 3 — write a row of $P$.** From $s = (1, 0)$ with $a = \text{right}$:
$P((1,1) \mid (1,0), \text{right}) = 1$, all other next states have
probability $0$.

**Step 4 — write a row of $R$.** $R((1,0), \text{right}, (1,1)) = 0$
(blank cell). But $R((2, 0), \text{right}, (2, 1)) = -1$ (lands on red),
and $R((1, 2), \text{down}, (2, 2)) = +1$ (lands on green).

**Step 5 — fix $\gamma$.** Pick $\gamma = 0.9$ for now; lesson 4 explains
the choice.

That is the entire MDP. It fits on one page.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** For the 3×3 grid above, write the **action space at the corner
$(0, 0)$**. Are any actions disallowed there?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Re-read Problem 2 of Discover.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = \{\text{up}, \text{down}, \text{left}, \text{right}\}$. Nothing is
*disallowed* — "up" and "left" simply leave the agent at $(0, 0)$ via the
bounce-back. Keeping $A$ the same at every state makes the maths uniform.
</details>

**W2.** From $(0, 1)$ with $a = \text{down}$ (deterministic), name $s'$ and
the reward $R(s, a, s')$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"down" means $(r, c) \mapsto (r+1, c)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s' = (1, 1)$, a blank cell. Reward $= 0$.
</details>

**W3.** Write the size of $S$ and $A$ for the 5×5 grid in the Touch widget.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|S| = 25$, $|A| = 4$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Switch on slippery floor (the rule from Problem 4). From the centre
$(1, 1)$ with action $\text{down}$, write the row of $P(s' \mid s, a)$ as a
small table. Confirm the row sums to 1.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Three possible landings: intended, left of intended, right of intended.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
"down" intends $(2, 1)$ (red). Left of intended: $(2, 0)$; right of
intended: $(2, 2)$.
$P((2,1) \mid (1,1), \text{down}) = 0.8$,
$P((2,0) \mid (1,1), \text{down}) = 0.1$,
$P((2,2) \mid (1,1), \text{down}) = 0.1$. Sum = 1.0.

Notice the slip can either save you (landing on green) or doom you (landing
on red). Stochastic transitions are why planning is non-trivial.
</details>

**P2.** Same slippery floor. With the rewards green = +1, red = −1, blank = 0,
compute the **expected immediate reward** for the same state-action of P1.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Expected reward $= \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) R(s, a, s')$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.8 \cdot (-1) + 0.1 \cdot 0 + 0.1 \cdot (+1) = -0.7$.

Even though the *intended* action heads straight onto red, the slip
contributes a +1 with probability 0.1, raising the expectation slightly.
</details>

**P3.** A second action **stay** is added to $A$: the agent does nothing,
$P(s \mid s, \text{stay}) = 1$, $R(s, \text{stay}, s) = 0$. Does this change
the *Markov property*? Why or why not?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
No. The Markov property says next-state probabilities depend only on the
current state-action pair, not history. Adding a new action that itself
satisfies that rule keeps the MDP Markov.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Modify the world.* Make a 4×4 grid with green at $(3, 3)$, red at
$(2, 2)$ and $(2, 3)$, and bounce-back walls. With deterministic movement,
write $P((s', s, a))$ for $s = (2, 1)$ and *all four* actions. Use a small
table.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Four rows, one per action; each row has a single entry equal to 1 because
movement is deterministic.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Action  | Next state $s'$ |
|---------|-----------------|
| up      | $(1, 1)$        |
| down    | $(3, 1)$        |
| left    | $(2, 0)$        |
| right   | $(2, 2)$ (red!) |

Each row of $P$ is a single 1 with the rest 0. The *right* action lands you
on red — the table itself does not punish you, but the *reward* function
will: $R((2,1), \text{right}, (2,2)) = -1$.
</details>

**S2.** Pick a real-world example that *fails* the Markov property as
written, then redesign the state to *make* it Markov. Aim for one paragraph.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Think of a card game. "Where the deck is now" depends on what has been
played.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Blackjack with one shoe is *not* Markov if the state is just "your current
hand total" — what comes next depends on which cards have already left the
deck. Fix it by enlarging the state to "(hand total, full count of
remaining cards)". Now next-card probabilities are fixed by the state
alone, and the process is Markov.

This is the design principle of lesson 2's key insight: when in doubt,
expand the state until the past stops mattering.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
Richard Bellman invented the term *dynamic programming* in 1953 at RAND
Corporation. He did not pick it for accuracy — he picked it because his
boss, Charles E. Wilson, "had a pathological fear and hatred of the word
*research*." Bellman wrote in his autobiography that *dynamic* sounded
"impossible to use in a pejorative sense" and would survive the budget
review. The maths in this lesson is one of the things he was hiding behind
that name.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"States are positions on the board."** Sometimes — in the grid world,
   yes. In general a *state* is whatever data is sufficient to predict the
   future of the system. In a card game it includes the deck composition;
   in a physics sim it includes velocities; in a video game it might
   include hidden timers. Position alone is rarely enough.
2. **"Reward is part of the agent."** No. Reward is part of the *world*,
   not the agent. The agent observes a reward; it does not choose its
   value. (The agent chooses its actions, hoping to *cause* large rewards.)
3. **"$\gamma$ is just a hyperparameter."** It looks like one, but it
   shapes what counts as "good." Lesson 4 will make this concrete: with
   $\gamma$ small, the agent is myopic; with $\gamma$ near 1, it plans
   long.
:::

### Takeaways

1. An [MDP](/glossary.qmd#markov-decision-process) is **five things**:
   states $S$, actions $A$, transition kernel $P$, reward $R$, discount
   $\gamma$. Pin those down and you have specified the world.
2. *Markov* means: the next state and reward depend only on the **current**
   state-action pair. No memory of the path is required — the state is the
   memory.
3. Stochastic transitions ($P$ is a distribution, not a function) are what
   make planning interesting. Without them, the agent's life is a fixed
   trajectory.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you, on a blank sheet, write down the five components of an MDP
   and say what each one is in plain English?
2. Did you complete W1–P3 without peeking at the *Full solution*?
3. Can you name a real-world process that is *not* Markov as written, and
   describe how you would redesign the state to make it Markov?

- **3 yes:** move on to lesson 2 (state transitions and the Markov
  property in detail).
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2 — they are the tightest test of "do I really
  see the structure?".
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover*. The five problems together build the
  whole definition.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **`t3.basic-probability`** — for $P(s' \mid s, a)$ as a row of a
  probability table summing to 1.
- **`t5.sets-logic-proofs`** — for the comfort with "let $S$ be the set
  of states" notation.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 2 — State Transitions and the Markov Property.** Zoom into $P$
  alone; show the Markov property is *load-bearing* for everything that
  follows.
- **Lesson 3 — Policies as Conditional Distributions.** Introduce the
  agent's strategy on top of the world.
- All later lessons in the track sit on top of this one. The MDP is the
  alphabet; the rest of the track is the literature.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every modern reinforcement-learning system — game-playing
agents (AlphaZero, MuZero), robotics control loops, dialogue policies in
RLHF, recommendation systems, traffic-signal control — frames its
environment as some flavour of MDP. The framework is the lingua franca of
sequential decision-making.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** NPC behaviour ("how should the goblin react when the
hero is two squares away?"), procedural difficulty ("how hard should the
next level be given the player's history?"), and matchmaking are all
decision processes that an MDP cleanly models.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / control.** Operations research (inventory control,
scheduling), epidemiology (vaccination policy under uncertainty), and
classical control theory (the LQR is a continuous-state MDP) all share
this skeleton. The five-tuple is older than RL by decades.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this track
expands across 38 lessons. Read it now for orientation; come back here
for depth.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), Chapter 1. Cite freely; never paste Zhao's prose.
- Touchstones honoured: Pestalozzi (the grid is the *Anschauung*), Russian
  problem-first (Discover builds the definition before *Name* states it),
  Better Explained ("five things" is the Aha! framing).
:::
